Ukraine air raid alert dashboard

Monthly alert frequency — all regions combined

Total alerts issued across Ukraine per month. The October 2022 spike (shown in red) followed mass missile strikes on infrastructure.
Monthly alerts.

Alerts by hour of day

Night hours (00:00–06:00) account for 58% of all alerts.
Hourly distribution.

Annual totals by year

2024 was the most intense year. The conflict entered a new phase with longer-range Shahed drone campaigns.
Annual totals.

Alerts by day of week

Attacks show no strong day-of-week preference, though Tuesday and Wednesday see slightly higher frequency.

Data sources: alerts.in.ua (official API) · Vadimkin dataset · air-alarms.in.ua. Data from Feb 24 – Mar 15, 2022 was collected from semi-automated unofficial sources and may contain minor inaccuracies.

Forecast model

A simple trend + seasonality model projects future alert patterns. This is a statistical estimate — actual alerts depend on military and political developments that no model can predict.

How this forecast works: Linear trend fitted on monthly totals, combined with a 12-month seasonal adjustment. Confidence intervals widen over time to reflect increasing uncertainty.
Region: Months ahead:

Historical + forecast — monthly alert count (solid = historical, dashed = projected)

Shaded area = 80% confidence interval. Forecast starts July 2026.
Forecast.

Forecast summary

Model assumptions & limitations

• The model assumes the conflict continues at roughly current intensity.

• Ceasefire or escalation would significantly alter actual numbers.

• Seasonal patterns from 2022–2025 are assumed to continue.

• This is a statistical model, not a military intelligence assessment.

Do not use this forecast for safety planning.

Methodology & data sources

How this analysis was built — written for a general audience with no technical background.

cell_tower Where does the data come from?

Ukraine maintains an official air alert system run through the Ministry of Defense. Alerts are tracked in near real-time by alerts.in.ua. The dataset compiled by Vadimkin on GitHub collects this data daily. We use official oblast-level alerts only — city-level OSINT alerts are excluded to avoid double counting.

bar_chart What counts as one "alert"?

One alert = one continuous siren period in one region. It begins when the siren sounds and ends when the all-clear is given. Note: from Feb 24 to Mar 15, 2022, data came from unofficial sources and may have minor inaccuracies.

map How are regions defined?

Ukraine has 24 oblasts plus the city of Kyiv as a separate unit. Occupied parts of Donetsk, Luhansk, Zaporizhzhia, and Kherson oblasts have limited coverage — alerts apply to the Ukrainian-controlled portions only.

auto_graph How is the forecast calculated?

(1) Trend: a linear fit on monthly totals. (2) Seasonality: average deviation for each calendar month (October tends to be higher than June). The forecast combines these two. The confidence band widens as uncertainty grows.

warning Important caveats

Alert counts are a proxy for threat intensity, not a count of missiles reaching targets. A single mass attack across all 25 regions creates a spike that looks similar to weeks of moderate activity. These numbers represent over 4 years of daily danger for Ukrainian civilians.

Primary sources: alerts.in.ua · air-alarms.in.ua · github.com/Vadimkin/ukrainian-air-raid-sirens-dataset · "Predictive analytics of air alerts in the Russian-Ukrainian war", arXiv 2411.14625 (2024).
